course_description:
The course provides an introduction to the basic processes and principles of public policy. This includes the making of policies and the implementation of agreed programs. The quality of decisions and the effectiveness of policy programs are vital in every country. The course examines the main components of the policy process – such as problem identification, debates about policy options, the involvement of non-government stakeholders, decision-making processes, implementation challenges, and the evaluation of program effectiveness. Relevant policy literature from Australia and internationally are considered.

This course starts by considering the basic nature of political activity relevant to public policymaking inside and outside governmental institutions.

The key actors or organisations involved are examined such as government ministers, their policy advisors, public servants in various governmental agencies, private corporations, the media, lobby groups, not-for-profit community organisations, charities, think tanks and research institutes.ﾠ

The focus of the course is to familiarise students with the main ways to analyse public policies as formulated in strategy documents, laws and regulations. Thus we deal with the theory and practice of policymaking, looking at the differences arising in specific policy issues and their institutional contexts. We discuss the role of leaders, ideologies, bureaucracies, expert evidence and the mass media. Students with experience outside Australia will be encouraged to report on that experience as a basis of comparison.ﾠ

We assess some of the main scholarly approaches to the study of public policy. We do this by analysing the main dimensions (or stages) identified in the policy literature. These include agenda-setting, choosing policy instruments, consultation, co-ordination, implementation and evaluation. Attention will also be given to well-known frameworks for understanding policy success/failure, and for explaining policy reform, policy transfer, and using evidence in complex problem-solving.

At every point in the course, students will be invited to consider the relevance of their own work experience, as well as the analytical literature focused on specific areas of policy analysis and debate.ﾠ
timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Provision is made for weekly pre-recorded lectures and in-person and online seminars, and regular attendance is highly recommended. Lecture materials (but not seminar discussions) are pre-recorded and available through Blackboard Ultra.
course_aims: POLS7111 is intended as an introduction to the UQ Graduate Program in Governance and Public Policy. The course also provides an introduction to policy and regulation issues for students in other program areas. The course provides basic knowledge about the contribution of political science perspectives to the study of public policy and regulation. It gives students many opportunities to apply key concepts and frameworks in order to understand and explain policy choices and to explore case studies. Selection of relevant policy case studies for the Case Study Report may be informed by previous work experience. Research tasks may includeﾠreviewing and evaluating policies and programs in a specific area of government activity.ﾠ

learning_outcomes.description: Explain how debates about ‘problems’ give rise to discussion of policy responses.
Analyse how policy decisions are made in relation to complex or controversial problems.
Map the influence of various actors/stakeholders in the shaping of problems and policies.
Write a professional research-based report on a policy issue drawing upon relevant policy literature.

assessment_summary.title: Conceptual Review A and B (1000 words + 20 references)
Policy Case Study Report (3000 words + references)

assessment_details.title: Conceptual Review A and B (1000 words + 20 references)
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 1 consists of two short writing tasks: Conceptual Review A (500 words + 10 references) due 20/3/2026 5.00pm. Conceptual Review A is valued at 10%. Conceptual Review B (500 words + 10 references) due 17/4/2026 5.00pm. Conceptual Review B is valued at 10%. Combined, Conceptual Review A and B are valued at 20%. About this assessment: Conceptual Review A and B For Conceptual Review A, students should write an answer to one question which is based on material covered in Week 1, 2, 3 and 4. Similar, for Conceptual Review B, students should also write an answer to one question which is based on material covered in Week 5, 6, 7 and 8. To answer the questions for Conceptual Review A and B, students are required to use the theories, concepts and models that have been covered in Week 2. For both Conceptual Review A and B, students will be provided a list of questions developed based on the relevant weekly topics they can choose from. These questions will be posted on Blackboard Ultra at the beginning of the semester. Answers are required to be 500 words (excluding in-text references and reference list) with a +10% tolerance allowed. Students must relate their answers to the policy literature discussed in the course as outlined further above. Students should not simply give their opinions or use internet summaries. Please note, the use of any Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) is forbidden and will constitute student misconduct. Students' answer should include 10 relevant references per Conceptual Review. For advice on referencing style, please see the POLSIS Essay Guide. ACADEMIC INTEGRITY - IMPORTANT! Turnitin software to detect plagiarism is automatically employed in the submission process to help examiners assess the originality of all written material. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic, and complex. While students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of this assessment will require critical engagement with specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard Ultra and using Turnitin .
In uploading an assignment via Turnitin you are certifying that it is your original work, that it has not been copied in whole or part from another person or source except where this is properly acknowledged, and that it has not in whole or part been previously submitted for assessment in any other course at this or any other university.
When you successfully submit your assessment to Turnitin after previewing the uploaded document (to make sure that you have chosen the correct file), you should see the “Submission Complete!” message. After this, a downloadable Digital Receipt will display on your Assignment Dashboard. It is your responsibility to download the Digital Receipt as proof of submission. Turnitin will not send this receipt to you automatically.
If you do not see the downloadable receipt on your assignment dashboard, you should regard your submission as unsuccessful.
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again, then email your course coordinator immediately.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Unless an extension is granted, penalties for late submission apply. Students are penalised 10% of the maximum possible mark allocated for the assessment item for every calendar day that an assessment item is late.
Marks will be deducted each day for up to 28 calendar days at which point the submitted item will not receive any marks unless an extension has been approved. Each 24-hour block is triggered from the time the submission is due.

assessment_details.title: Policy Case Study Report (3000 words + references)
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 2 consists of a longer writing task: Policy Case Study Report (3000 words + references) due 8/5/2026 5.00pm. The Policy Case Study Report is valued at 40%. About this assessment: Policy Case Study Report For the Policy Case Study Report, a list of suitable research topics will be provided on Blackboard Ultra early in the semester. Students however may negotiate a variation of these to suit their interest. As a part of this assessment, student should discuss their selected topic with the Course Convener. This can be done either in-person before and/or after the seminar or in writing via the Course Convener's UQ staff e-mail (a.jedzini@uq.edu.au). Students' Policy Case Study Report will analyse a selected policy problem, decision or trend, by relating it to wider theories and issues about the policymaking process as discussed extensively in the course. The Policy Case Study Report should aim to connect policy theory to the policy example students have chosen. In selecting a policy issue or policy problem or policy process, students need to ensure that their topic is not only of interest to themselves, but is also likely to have a sufficient range of material available for students to provide an in-depth analysis of the actors, processes and context in which the policy decision or policy outcome was produced. The analytical task is to explain what happened and why in relation to students' policy example. Where practicable, students might consider using both primary sources (e.g. government documents) and secondary sources (e.g. commentaries by scholars, stakeholders and media analysts). The conceptual task is to connect students' analysis of their problem or policy case to the broader themes and theories discussed in the course. Simply describing what happened is not enough. Please note, the use of any Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) is forbidden and will constitute student misconduct. The Policy Case Study Report should be clearly structured and properly referenced (approximately 10 references per 1,000 words) using the POLSIS Essay Guide. The word limit is 3000 words (excluding in-text references, reference list and appendix) with a +10% tolerance. If relevant, it is acceptable to attach a short documentary or statistical appendix to back up an argument. This will not itself be the subject to assessment. ACADEMIC INTEGRITY - IMPORTANT! Turnitin software to detect plagiarism is automatically employed in the submission process to help examiners assess the originality of all written material. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic, and complex. While students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of this assessment will require critical engagement with specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Oral Assessment
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 3 is an Oral Assessment The oral assessment will be held in the first week of the exam period at the end of semester. It will run for ten minutes and be held one-to-one with a member of the teaching team. About this assessment: For the Oral Assessment, students are required to respond to two questions covering key topics addressed in the course. Indicative topics will be discussed in the Week 13 Seminar. In the first five minutes, students will respond to a question on a topic of their choosing. In the second five minutes, they will respond to a topic chosen by the marker. For each student, one topic chosen will address the content of Week 3, 4, 5, 6 and 7, the other will address the content of Weeks 8, 9, 11, 12 and 13. Further information on the topics, conditions and marking criteria will be provided via the course Blackboard. Additional time for preparation will also be provided in the Week 13 Seminar. Note that all assessments will be recorded for the purposes of appeal. Scheduling: Students will be allocated a specific day and time in that week well in advance and these will be communicated via Blackboard and email. While there are no extensions allowed for this assessment - they must all be done in the first week of the exam period - students who have access plans or exceptional circumstances (with documentation) should contact the course coordinator if the allotted day or time slot needs to be changed. Students with access plans should contact the course coordinator as soon as possible if they require a modification to be made to the assessment. Accommodations in response to access plans are available (via, for example, extending the time given for the test as a whole or its components). ACADEMIC INTEGRITY - IMPORTANT! This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Students who have access plans or exceptional circumstances (with documentation) should contact the course coordinator if the allotted day or time slot needs to be changed.

assessment_details.title: Participation and Engagement
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 4 consists of a participation and engagement task: Consistent in-class participation and active engagement from Week 1 to Week 13. Participation and Engagement is valued at 10%. About this assessment: Participation and Engagement Students, whether in the on-campus internal class or in the online external class, are expected to consistently attend and participate in classes and engage in small-group discussions and larger collaborative class activities. One mark is awarded for each active class participation and engagement up to a maximum of 10 marks. Unable to attend class: Please email the Course Convener Lecturer Miss Anne Nygaard Jedzini at a.jedzini@uq.edu.au if you cannot make it to class. There are no replacement assessment items for Participation and Engagement. ACADEMIC INTEGRITY - IMPORTANT! learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Introduction, Course Overview and Key Themes This is the first week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to the course outline, content including the central ideas of public policy and requirements of assessments. More specifically, the lecture will provide an introduction to key topics such as how to define and conceptualise public policy, how public policy is conducted across liberal democracies, who gets to influence and participate in public policymaking and how, why particular public policy decisions are made and how, and the extent to which different public policy instruments lead to certain public policy outcomes. In this week's seminar, students will be introduced to each other for the first time, have the opportunity to learn more and ask questions about the course outline and content, and upcoming assessments. Finally, students will also be doing small-group discussions and activities on the topics covered in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01

Theories and Models for Understanding Public Policy This is the second week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to the relevant theories, concepts and models of public policy. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of how public policy is theorised and conceptualised in the academic literature, and how different theories and concepts help making sense of public policy issues and decisions. The lecture will also cover the boundaries including limitations of public policy theories and concepts, and what the empirical implications of these are for understanding public policy in practise. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Tackling Complex and Wicked Problems This is the third week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to how complex and wicked problems are defined and conceptualised, and how they can be distinguished from other public policy issues. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of what complex and wicked problems are, how they can be identified, and how they can be understood. The lecture will also cover what factors that generate complex and wicked problems and what the solutions to these might be. The lecture will additionally cover what the real-life implications of complex and wicked problems are for public policymakers and other policy decision-makers, and what strategies they might choose to confront and address such problems. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Agenda-Setting, Media and Issue Framing This is the fourth week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to how public policy issues are identified and framed to influence the policy agenda. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of what policy agenda-setting is and who has the capacity and/or resources to set the policy agenda, the importance of agenda-setting to the public policymaking process, how agenda-setting informs what public policy issues are selected or not, how public policy issues help illuminating bigger underlying wicked problems, and the way in which public policy issues potentially offer feasible and legitimate solutions to address these wicked problems. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Participation, Consultation and Stakeholder Engagement This is the fifth week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to the aims and broader rationales for including every-day citizens, communities and stakeholders in the making of the policy decisions that affect them. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of the historical origins of participation and engagement in democratic governance, different models for engaging citizens, communities and stakeholders in policymaking, the strengths and short-comings of community engagement, and how to empower policymakers with the appropriate knowledge and tools to identify the most suitable methods of community engagement. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Selection and Application of Policy Instruments This is the sixth week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to what policy instruments are and how they are applied in practice. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of the relationship between policy goals and policy instruments, the different types of policy instruments available to policymakers, the ways in which policy instruments are central to policy design, and the importance of conditions for policy instruments to be effective. The lecture will also cover the relevance of historical context in the development and implementation of policy instruments. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
UQ Mid-Semester Break (1 Week) Please note, due to the university wide break, there is no seminar this week.

Policy Implementation and Coordination This is the seventh week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to what public policy implementation and coordination is, and how it relates to public services and the delivery of these. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of how public policy implementation is different from the making of policy, different models of public policy implementation, what is considered successful and unsuccessful public policy implementation, and what conditions that are necessary for public policy implementation to be effective. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Policy Success and Failure This is the eight week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to how to identify and understand policy success in liberal democracies, and the importance of evaluating policies in this context. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of the aims of evaluating policies, the theoretical and conceptual frameworks for evaluating policies, what aspects of policies that can or cannot be evaluated, and the criteria for determining what is considered policy success and failures. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Policy Transfer and Adaptation This is the ninth week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to how public policies can be transferred and adopted across different political and institutional contexts. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of where public policy ideas come from, the boundaries of and the extent to which public policies can transfer through time and place, why some public policies are more popular to transfer than others, and the real-life implications for those who are impacted by public policy transfers. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Pause Week for Case Study Report-Writing (No Seminar) Please note, this is a course specific pause week for students to write on their Case Study Report. This means there is no seminar this week. Learning outcomes: L04

Evidence-Based Policymaking This is the eleventh week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to what the role of evidence-based policymaking is in today's public policy landscape, and how evidence-based policymaking can improve policy decision-making and public policy outcomes. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of what evidence-based policy making is and how the concept originated, what the empirical and practical lessons of evidence-based policymaking are, what types of knowledge and/or expertise that is considered evidence-based (and therefore valuable), and the extent to which evidence-based policymaking aligns with what works in practice. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Crisis, Innovation and Reform This is the twelfth week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to what policy innovation is, and how this concept is related to policy crisis and policy reform more broadly. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of how to distinguish policy innovation from other public policy tools, the public policy issues policy innovation tackles or not, the political and institutional drivers and barriers to policy innovation, and what conditions that enable policy innovation to take root. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Policy Work, Policy Advice and Policy Capacity This is the thirteenth and final week of the course. In this week's pre-recorded lecture, students will be introduced to the skills and tools that typically are required for people working with public policy. More specifically, the lecture will provide an overview of how to build policy capacity in different areas of public policymaking, and how this relates to the concept of the policy advisory system. The lecture will also cover the nature of policy learning and the opportunities and challenges for people working as policy advisors. Given this is the last seminar, students will be discussing their experiences of their participation in the course. In this week's seminar, students will have the opportunity to engage in discussions and activities that address these topics in the pre-recorded lecture. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
